figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='20' y1='125' x2='220' y2='125' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M30 125 C70 125 90 30 120 30 C150 30 170 125 210 125' fill='none' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M120 30 C150 30 170 110 185 125 L120 125 Z' fill='rgba(16,185,129,0.22)'/><line x1='120' y1='30' x2='120' y2='125' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='185' y1='125' x2='185' y2='112' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='114' y='140' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>0</text><text x='180' y='140' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>+2</text></svg>

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: The Newton–Raphson iteration for solving $f(x)=0$ is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Linearise $f$ at $x_n$ and find where the tangent meets the axis.

**Working.**
$$x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Answer.** $x_{n+1}=x_n-\dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Applying one Newton–Raphson step to $f(x)=x^{2}-2$ with $x_0=1$ gives $x_1=$ _____ (round to 2 decimals).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: The bisection method can be applied on $[a,b]$ to find a root of $f$ only if
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees a root when $f$ changes sign.

**Working.** A sign change requires $f(a)\,f(b)<0$.

**Answer.** $f(a)\,f(b)<0$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For $f(x)=x^{2}-2$ on $[1,2]$, the first bisection mid-point is _____ .

figure.caption: Bisection on [1, 2] for f(x) = x^2 - 2: f(1) < 0 and f(2) > 0, so the first mid-point is 1.5.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='30' y1='70' x2='230' y2='70' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='60' x2='40' y2='80' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='60' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='58' x2='130' y2='82' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><text x='34' y='98' font-size='10' fill='#6366f1'>a = 1</text><text x='208' y='98' font-size='10' fill='#6366f1'>b = 2</text><text x='112' y='52' font-size='10' fill='#ef4444'>c = 1.5</text><text x='34' y='48' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>f < 0</text><text x='206' y='48' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>f > 0</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mid-point $c=\dfrac{a+b}{2}$.

**Working.**
$$c=\frac{1+2}{2}=1.5$$

**Answer.** $1.5$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Using the trapezoidal rule with $2$ sub-intervals ($h=1$), the estimate of $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}x^{2}\,dx$ is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $T=\dfrac{h}{2}\big[f_0+2f_1+f_2\big]$.

**Working.** $f(0)=0,\,f(1)=1,\,f(2)=4$.
$$T=\frac{1}{2}\big[0+2(1)+4\big]=3$$

**Answer.** $3$ (the exact value is $\tfrac{8}{3}$; the trapezoidal rule over-estimates here).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Simpson's $\tfrac{1}{3}$ rule requires the number of sub-intervals to be
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Simpson's 1/3 rule fits a parabola over each pair of sub-intervals.

**Working.** Pairing requires an even number of sub-intervals.

**Answer.** Even.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Using Simpson's $\tfrac{1}{3}$ rule with $2$ sub-intervals ($h=1$), the estimate of $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}x^{2}\,dx$ is _____ (round to 2 decimals).

figure.caption: Simpson's 1/3 rule fits a parabola through three equally spaced points to approximate the area.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='30' y1='160' x2='220' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M40 150 Q120 20 200 60' fill='rgba(99,102,241,0.12)' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='40' y2='150' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='120' y1='160' x2='120' y2='75' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='200' y1='160' x2='200' y2='60' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='40' cy='150' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><circle cx='120' cy='75' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><circle cx='200' cy='60' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='34' y='174' font-size='9' fill='#94a3b8'>x0</text><text x='114' y='174' font-size='9' fill='#94a3b8'>x1</text><text x='194' y='174' font-size='9' fill='#94a3b8'>x2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $S=\dfrac{h}{3}\big[f_0+4f_1+f_2\big]$.

**Working.** $f(0)=0,\,f(1)=1,\,f(2)=4$.
$$S=\frac{1}{3}\big[0+4(1)+4\big]=\frac{8}{3}\approx 2.67$$
Simpson's rule is exact for quadratics, matching the true value.

**Answer.** $\tfrac{8}{3}\approx 2.67$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Euler's method for $y'=f(x,y)$ advances the solution by

figure.caption: Euler's method: straight-line steps of slope f(x, y) approximate the true solution curve.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='30' y1='160' x2='220' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M30 150 C90 150 140 60 210 35' fill='none' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2'/><polyline points='30,150 80,138 130,112 180,70' fill='none' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='30' cy='150' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><circle cx='80' cy='138' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><circle cx='130' cy='112' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><circle cx='180' cy='70' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='150' y='45' font-size='10' fill='#10b981'>true</text><text x='110' y='135' font-size='10' fill='#ef4444'>Euler</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Forward Euler uses the slope at the current point.

**Working.**
$$y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(x_n,y_n)$$

**Answer.** $y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(x_n,y_n)$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For $y'=y$ with $y(0)=1$ and step $h=0.1$, one Euler step gives $y(0.1)\approx$ _____ (round to 2 decimals).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Compared with the classical fourth-order Runge–Kutta method, the forward Euler method has local accuracy of order
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Order of accuracy.

**Working.** Euler is a first-order method; RK4 is fourth-order, hence much more accurate per step.

**Answer.** Lower order (first order).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For $y'=y$ with $y(0)=1$ and step $h=0.1$, TWO Euler steps give $y(0.2)\approx$ _____ (round to 2 decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Apply forward Euler twice.

**Working.**
$$y_1=1+0.1(1)=1.1,\qquad y_2=1.1+0.1(1.1)=1.21$$

**Answer.** $1.21$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Gaussian elimination transforms a coefficient matrix into
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Forward elimination produces zeros below the pivots.

**Working.** The result is an upper-triangular system solved by back-substitution.

**Answer.** An upper-triangular matrix.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Given $f(1)=2$ and $f(3)=8$, linear interpolation estimates $f(2)\approx$ _____ .

figure.caption: Linear interpolation between (1, 2) and (3, 8) estimates f(2) = 5 (the midpoint of the chord).
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='30' y1='160' x2='220' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='60' y1='130' x2='190' y2='40' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='130' r='3' fill='#10b981'/><circle cx='190' cy='40' r='3' fill='#10b981'/><circle cx='125' cy='85' r='3' fill='#ef4444'/><line x1='125' y1='160' x2='125' y2='85' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='48' y='126' font-size='9' fill='#10b981'>(1, 2)</text><text x='176' y='36' font-size='9' fill='#10b981'>(3, 8)</text><text x='130' y='80' font-size='9' fill='#ef4444'>(2, 5)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Linear interpolation $f(x)\approx f_0+\dfrac{f_1-f_0}{x_1-x_0}(x-x_0)$.

**Working.**
$$f(2)\approx 2+\frac{8-2}{3-1}(2-1)=2+3=5$$

**Answer.** $5$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Which of the following are INTERPOLATION techniques?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Distinguish interpolation from integration and root-finding.

**Working.**
- Lagrange — interpolation (TRUE).
- Newton's divided difference — interpolation (TRUE).
- Simpson's rule — numerical integration (FALSE).
- Newton–Raphson — root finding (FALSE).

**Answer.** Options (a) and (b).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Two Newton–Raphson iterations on $f(x)=x^{2}-2$ with $x_0=1$ give $x_2=$ _____ (round to 2 decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Iterate $x_{n+1}=x_n-\dfrac{x_n^{2}-2}{2x_n}$.

**Working.**
$$x_1=1.5,\qquad x_2=1.5-\frac{2.25-2}{3}=1.5-0.0833=1.4167\approx 1.42$$

**Answer.** $\approx 1.42$ (converging to $\sqrt2$).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Near a simple root, the Newton–Raphson method converges
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Convergence rate of Newton's method.

**Working.** For a simple root the error roughly squares each step — quadratic convergence.

**Answer.** Quadratically.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: From the tabulated values $f(0)=1,\;f(1)=3,\;f(2)=5$, the trapezoidal estimate of $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}f\,dx$ (with $h=1$) is _____ .

figure.caption: Two trapezoidal strips approximating the area under f from x = 0 to x = 2.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='25' y1='135' x2='205' y2='135' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='25' y1='15' x2='25' y2='135' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='25,135 25,115 105,75 105,135' fill='rgba(99,102,241,0.16)' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='105,135 105,75 185,55 185,135' fill='rgba(16,185,129,0.16)' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='20' y='150' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>0</text><text x='100' y='150' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>1</text><text x='180' y='150' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>2</text></svg>

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: The quantity $|\text{true value}-\text{approximate value}|$ is termed the
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Error definitions.

**Working.** The plain magnitude of the difference is the absolute error; dividing by the true value gives the relative error.

**Answer.** Absolute error.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For $f(x)=x^{2}-2$ on $[1,2]$, the SECOND bisection mid-point is _____ (round to 2 decimals).

figure.caption: Bisection: the sign change at the first mid-point keeps the bracket [1, 1.5].
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='20' y1='70' x2='220' y2='70' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='40' cy='70' r='4' fill='#10b981'/><circle cx='200' cy='70' r='4' fill='#ef4444'/><circle cx='120' cy='70' r='4' fill='#6366f1'/><text x='34' y='90' font-size='10' fill='#10b981'>1</text><text x='196' y='90' font-size='10' fill='#ef4444'>2</text><text x='112' y='58' font-size='10' fill='#6366f1'>1.5</text><line x1='40' y1='100' x2='120' y2='100' stroke='#f59e0b' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='114' font-size='9' fill='#f59e0b'>new bracket</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Keep the half that contains the sign change, then bisect again.

**Working.** $f(1.5)=0.25>0$ and $f(1)=-1<0$, so the root lies in $[1,1.5]$.
$$c_2=\frac{1+1.5}{2}=1.25$$

**Answer.** $1.25$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Which of the following are methods for finding ROOTS of equations?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Root-finding vs numerical integration.

**Working.**
- Bisection, Newton–Raphson, secant — all root-finding (TRUE).
- Trapezoidal rule — numerical integration (FALSE).

**Answer.** Options (a), (b) and (c).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Applying one Newton–Raphson step to $f(x)=x^{3}-x-1$ with $x_0=1$ gives $x_1=$ _____ (round to 2 decimals).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Which statements about Simpson's $\tfrac13$ rule are TRUE?
options: It requires an even number of sub-intervals
It fits parabolas through groups of three points
It is exact for polynomials up to degree $3$
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Properties of Simpson's 1/3 rule.

**Working.**
- Even number of sub-intervals — TRUE.
- Parabolic fit over point triples — TRUE.
- Exact up to cubic polynomials — TRUE.
- It needs EQUALLY spaced points — FALSE.

**Answer.** Options (a), (b) and (c).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Using Simpson's $\tfrac13$ rule with $2$ sub-intervals ($h=0.5$), the estimate of $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}e^{x}\,dx$ is _____ (round to 2 decimals).

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Estimating a value OUTSIDE the range of the given data points is called
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Interpolation vs extrapolation.

**Working.** Interpolation estimates within the data range; estimating beyond it is extrapolation.

**Answer.** Extrapolation.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Given $f(2)=4$ and $f(4)=16$, linear interpolation estimates $f(3)\approx$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Linear interpolation at the mid-point averages the endpoints.

**Working.**
$$f(3)\approx \frac{4+16}{2}=10$$
(The true value of $x^{2}$ is $9$; linear interpolation introduces a small error.)

**Answer.** $10$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: In the Newton–Raphson method (see figure), the next estimate $x_{n+1}$ is the point where
options: the chord between $x_n$ and $x_{n-1}$ meets the axis

figure.caption: Newton-Raphson: the tangent at x_n intersects the x-axis at the next estimate.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='205' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M30 140 Q120 -10 195 30' fill='none' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='55' x2='95' y2='120' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='1.6'/><circle cx='150' cy='55' r='3' fill='#6366f1'/><line x1='150' y1='55' x2='150' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='150' y='134' font-size='10' fill='#94a3b8'>x_n</text><text x='86' y='134' font-size='10' fill='#ef4444'>x_n+1</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The iteration follows the tangent line to the axis.

**Working.** The tangent at $(x_n,f(x_n))$ meets the $x$-axis at $x_{n+1}=x_n-\dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.

**Answer.** The tangent at $x_n$ meets the $x$-axis.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Compute the determinant of the $2\times2$ matrix shown.

figure.caption: 2x2 matrix
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><text x='25' y='52' font-size='16'>[</text><text x='45' y='45' font-size='12'>3   2</text><text x='45' y='70' font-size='12'>1   4</text><text x='98' y='52' font-size='16'>]</text><text x='25' y='100' font-size='9'>find determinant</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\det\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}=ad-bc$.

**Working.**
$$\det=3(4)-2(1)=12-2=10$$

**Answer.** $10$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For the diagonal matrix shown, find the largest eigenvalue.

figure.caption: Diagonal matrix
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><text x='25' y='52' font-size='16'>[</text><text x='45' y='45' font-size='12'>2   0</text><text x='45' y='70' font-size='12'>0   3</text><text x='98' y='52' font-size='16'>]</text><text x='25' y='100' font-size='9'>eigenvalues of diagonal matrix</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The eigenvalues of a diagonal matrix are its diagonal entries.

**Working.**
$$\lambda_1=2,\quad\lambda_2=3\Rightarrow\lambda_{max}=3$$

**Answer.** $3$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Find the largest eigenvalue of the symmetric matrix shown.

figure.caption: Symmetric matrix
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><text x='25' y='52' font-size='16'>[</text><text x='45' y='45' font-size='12'>4   1</text><text x='45' y='70' font-size='12'>1   4</text><text x='98' y='52' font-size='16'>]</text><text x='25' y='100' font-size='9'>largest eigenvalue</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For $\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\b&a\end{bmatrix}$ the eigenvalues are $a\pm b$.

**Working.**
$$\lambda=4\pm1=5,\,3\Rightarrow\lambda_{max}=5$$

**Answer.** $5$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Compute the shaded area $\int_0^2 x\,dx$ shown under the line $y=x$.

figure.caption: Area under y=x

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\int_0^2 x\,dx=\left[\dfrac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^2$.

**Working.**
$$\int_0^2 x\,dx=\frac{2^2}{2}=2$$

**Answer.** $2$.

figure.caption: Area under y=3x^2

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For the curve $y=x^2$ shown, compute the slope of the tangent at $x=2$.

figure.caption: Tangent to y=x^2
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='190' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='130' x2='100' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M40 40 Q100 150 160 40' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='110' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='90' r='3' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='40' y='140' font-size='8'>tangent at x=2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Slope $=\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2x$.

**Working.**
$$\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=2}=2(2)=4$$

**Answer.** $4$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Using the trapezoidal rule with the three ordinates $f_0=1$, $f_1=2$, $f_2=5$ at spacing $h=1$, estimate the integral.

figure.caption: Ordinates for numerical integration
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='200' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='40' y1='120' x2='40' y2='110' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='120' x2='110' y2='90' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='120' x2='180' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='35' y='105' font-size='8'>1</text><text x='105' y='85' font-size='8'>2</text><text x='175' y='35' font-size='8'>5</text><text x='40' y='140' font-size='8'>ordinates, h=1</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Trapezoidal rule: $I=\dfrac{h}{2}(f_0+2f_1+f_2)$.

**Working.**
$$I=\frac{1}{2}(1+2(2)+5)=\frac{1}{2}(10)=5$$

**Answer.** $5$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Using Simpson's one-third rule with the same ordinates $f_0=1$, $f_1=2$, $f_2=5$ at $h=1$, estimate the integral (two decimals).

figure.caption: Ordinates for Simpson's rule

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: From the Venn diagram, $P(A)=0.5$, $P(B)=0.4$ and $P(A\cap B)=0.2$. Compute $P(A\cup B)$.

figure.caption: Venn diagram of two events
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='85' cy='70' r='45' fill='#bfdbfe' fill-opacity='0.6' stroke='#2563eb'/><circle cx='135' cy='70' r='45' fill='#fca5a5' fill-opacity='0.6' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='52' y='75' font-size='9'>0.5</text><text x='150' y='75' font-size='9'>0.4</text><text x='100' y='75' font-size='9'>0.2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B)$.

**Working.**
$$P(A\cup B)=0.5+0.4-0.2=0.7$$

**Answer.** $0.7$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: On the probability tree shown, the first branch has probability $0.6$ and the chosen second branch has conditional probability $0.5$. Compute the probability of the path.

figure.caption: Probability tree

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Multiply probabilities along a path.

**Working.**
$$P=0.6\times0.5=0.3$$

**Answer.** $0.3$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For the normal (Gaussian) distribution shown, the proportion of area lying within one standard deviation of the mean is approximately:

figure.caption: Normal curve with +/- 1 sigma band
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M30 110 Q110 -10 190 110' fill='#bfdbfe' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='85' y1='110' x2='85' y2='30' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='135' y1='110' x2='135' y2='30' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='65' y='128' font-size='8'>mean +/- 1 sigma</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The empirical (68-95-99.7) rule for the normal distribution.

**Reasoning.** About 68% of the area lies within $\pm1\sigma$, 95% within $\pm2\sigma$ and 99.7% within $\pm3\sigma$.

**Answer.** About 68%.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Compute the arithmetic mean of the four data values shown in the bar chart: $2,4,6,8$.

figure.caption: Bar chart of four values
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='45' y='90' width='25' height='20'/><rect x='80' y='70' width='25' height='40'/><rect x='115' y='50' width='25' height='60'/><rect x='150' y='30' width='25' height='80'/></g><text x='48' y='125' font-size='8'>2, 4, 6, 8</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\bar{x}=\dfrac{\sum x}{n}$.

**Working.**
$$\bar{x}=\frac{2+4+6+8}{4}=\frac{20}{4}=5$$

**Answer.** $5$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For the data $2,4,6$ (mean shown dashed), compute the population standard deviation (two decimals).

figure.caption: Data with mean line
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='55' y='80' width='25' height='30'/><rect x='100' y='60' width='25' height='50'/><rect x='145' y='40' width='25' height='70'/></g><line x1='30' y1='70' x2='200' y2='70' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='50' y='125' font-size='8'>2, 4, 6 (mean dashed)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\sigma=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x-\bar{x})^2}{n}}$ with $\bar{x}=4$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma=\sqrt{\frac{(2-4)^2+(4-4)^2+(6-4)^2}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{8}{3}}=1.63$$

**Answer.** $1.63$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For the vectors $\mathbf{a}=(3,4)$ and $\mathbf{b}=(1,2)$ shown, compute the dot product $\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}$.

figure.caption: Two vectors
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='120' y2='90' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#v)'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='60' y2='60' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#v)'/><defs><marker id='v' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='38' y='140' font-size='8'>a=(3,4), b=(1,2)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=a_xb_x+a_yb_y$.

**Working.**
$$\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=3(1)+4(2)=3+8=11$$

**Answer.** $11$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Compute the magnitude of the vector $(3,4)$ shown.

figure.caption: Vector magnitude
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='140' y2='50' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#vm)'/><defs><marker id='vm' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='80' y='100' font-size='8'>(3,4)</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $|\mathbf{v}|=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$.

**Working.**
$$|\mathbf{v}|=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=\sqrt{25}=5$$

**Answer.** $5$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For $\mathbf{a}=(3,4)$ and $\mathbf{b}=(1,2)$, compute the magnitude of the cross product $|\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}|$ (the parallelogram area).

figure.caption: Parallelogram spanned by two vectors
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><polygon points='30,120 120,90 150,30 60,60' fill='#fde68a' fill-opacity='0.5'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='120' y2='90' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#cr)'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='60' y2='60' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#cr)'/><defs><marker id='cr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='40' y='140' font-size='8'>parallelogram area</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** In 2D, $|\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}|=|a_xb_y-a_yb_x|$.

**Working.**
$$|\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}|=|3(2)-4(1)|=|6-4|=2$$

**Answer.** $2$.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: The solution curve shown satisfies $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=ky$. Its general solution is:

figure.caption: Exponential solution curve
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='190' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><path d='M30 115 Q120 110 170 30' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='60' font-size='8'>dy/dx = k y</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=ky$ is a separable, first-order linear ODE.

**Reasoning.** Separating variables, $\int\dfrac{dy}{y}=\int k\,dx\Rightarrow\ln y=kx+c$, so $y=Ce^{kx}$, the exponential growth/decay curve shown.

**Answer.** $y=Ce^{kx}$.
